frame_label: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha-Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment: Priya beta, bura mat maanna, par shaadi-shuda aurat ke maang mein thoda sindoor rahe to hi suhagan jaisi shobha aati hai. Aajkal ki ladkiyan isse chhoti baat samajh leti hain, lekin yeh hamari parampara hai aur pati ke mangal se bhi juda hota hai. Bahar nikalte waqt dhyan rakha karo, mohalla waale bhi dekhte hain.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is covert misogyny because it frames visible marital markers as tradition, auspiciousness, and a married woman's proper appearance rather than direct control. It encodes pressure on a married woman to wear sindoor consistently and invokes social judgment and husband-welfare symbolism.
original_frame: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
persona_text: You are Kamla. Age 62. A woman in a joint family in Varanasi. Your daughter in law Priya has been married to your son for eight months. Priya wears sindoor on most days but sometimes comes downstairs in the morning without it Ñ usually when she is rushing to work. This has happened four times in the past two months. You have not said anything directly. But today she came down without it and your neighbour was sitting in the living room having chai. You are now in the kitchen with Priya after the neighbour has left. You are telling Priya what you noticed and what the neighbour must have thought and what it means for a married woman to step outside without sindoor and what people in this mohalla say about women who do not observe these things properly.
